content.problemStatement:
The way you counted the number of paths in the previous problem is called the ***Rule of Product***. 

Formally, if there are $A$ ways to perform one task and $B$ ways to perform a second task, and these tasks can be done independently of each other, then the total number of ways to perform both tasks is $A$ $\cdot$ $B$


In the previous example, no matter the path you choose for $A$, there always existed 2 paths from $A$ to $B$ - so these two actions were independent.

Again, this can be extended to more than 2 tasks. 

So, if we have $N$ independent tasks and $i$-th task can be performed in $A_i$ then there are $\prod_{i=1}^N A_i$ number of ways to perform all the tasks.


**Note:**: Product notation (also called pi notation) indicates repeated multiplication.

$\prod_{i=1}^N A_i$ = $A_1$ x $A_2$ x $A_3$......x $A_N$
constraints:
- $1 \leq T \leq 1000$
- $2 \leq N \leq 10^5$
- $1 \leq A_i \leq 10^9$
- The sum of $N$ across all test cases does not exceed $10^5$
content.inputFormat:
- The first line of input will contain a single integer $T$, denoting the number of test cases. The description of the test cases follows.
- The first line of each test case contains a single integer $N$, denoting the length of array $A$.
- The second line of each test case contains $N$ space-separated integers $A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_N$ — denoting the array $A$.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output on a new line the maximum value of $A_1+A_N$ you can get after several right rotations.
